title: Weak Session Management
description: Learn how weak session handling in web applications can allow attackers to hijack active user sessions and impersonate legitimate users.

attack_stages.title: Session Creation
attack_stages.description: The website creates a session to keep a user logged in after authentication.

attack_stages.title: Weak Session Protection
attack_stages.description: Session identifiers are not securely generated or protected.

attack_stages.title: Session Interception
attack_stages.description: An attacker obtains or predicts a valid session token.

attack_stages.title: Session Hijacking
attack_stages.description: The attacker uses the stolen session to impersonate a legitimate user.

attack_stages.title: Unauthorized Actions
attack_stages.description: The attacker performs actions as the victim user.

prevention_points.title: Secure Session IDs
prevention_points.description: Generate long, random, and unpredictable session identifiers.

prevention_points.title: Secure Cookie Settings
prevention_points.description: Use HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite cookie flags to protect session data.

prevention_points.title: Session Expiration
prevention_points.description: Expire sessions after inactivity or a defined time limit.

prevention_points.title: Session Invalidation
prevention_points.description: Immediately invalidate sessions upon logout or suspicious activity.

nodes.title: Scenario
nodes.text: A web application keeps users logged in using session-based authentication after they enter their credentials.

nodes.title: What is a Session?
nodes.text: A session is a mechanism used by web applications to remember a user after they log in, allowing them to stay authenticated across multiple requests.

nodes.title: Weak Session Management
nodes.text: Weak session management occurs when session identifiers are predictable, poorly protected, or not properly invalidated, making it easier for attackers to take over active sessions.

nodes.title: Attacker Goal
nodes.text: The attacker wants to gain control of an active user session in order to impersonate a legitimate user without knowing their password.

nodes.title: What is a likely consequence of weak session management?

choices.text: An attacker may hijack an active user session
The firewall blocks all session activity

nodes.title: Try Again
nodes.text: Weak session management affects how user sessions are created and protected, allowing attackers to reuse or steal valid session tokens.

nodes.title: Session Hijacking
nodes.text: The attacker successfully obtains or predicts a valid session token and uses it to impersonate the victim user.

nodes.title: Impact
nodes.text: Session hijacking can lead to unauthorized actions such as account changes, data theft, and unauthorized transactions performed under the victim's identity.

nodes.title: How to Prevent This
nodes.text: Organizations should generate secure session identifiers, use secure cookie settings (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), expire sessions after inactivity, and invalidate sessions immediately after logout.

nodes.title: Attack Replay
nodes.text: Review how a weak session mechanism allowed an attacker to take over an active user session and impersonate a legitimate user.

nodes.title: Scenario Complete
nodes.text: Weak session management allows attackers to hijack active sessions and impersonate users. Strong session handling practices are essential to protect authenticated users.
